correct_explanation: Explicit schema specification in the system prompt eliminates ambiguity by giving Claude a concrete, mandatory contract to fulfill on every call. When the exact field names, structure, and value types are declared, Claude has no basis to fall back on prose — the format requirement is unambiguous. This directly addresses the root cause: Claude was inferring format from context, which is probabilistic, not deterministic.

distractor_explanations.A: Few-shot examples improve consistency but don't guarantee it — Claude can still deviate when a ticket is ambiguous or unusual. This is a probabilistic nudge, not a structural constraint, so the ~10% failure rate may shrink but won't reach zero.
distractor_explanations.C: Vague instructions like 'structured format' leave the definition of structure entirely to Claude's inference, which is exactly the failure mode already occurring. Relying on a 'general tendency' accepts non-deterministic output for a system that requires deterministic output.
distractor_explanations.D: Moving enforcement to the parser treats the symptom rather than the cause, and writing a parser that handles both JSON and arbitrary prose is a fragile, high-maintenance solution. It also means ticket data lost in malformed prose responses is never recovered.

correct_explanation: When a prompt omits explicit format instructions, Claude infers a reasonable output format from context—but 'reasonable' is not the same as 'consistent.' Without a specified schema, Claude may alternate between prose, partial JSON, or varied key naming across responses, producing outputs that are semantically correct but structurally unpredictable. Downstream parsers expecting a strict schema will break on any deviation from the expected structure.

distractor_explanations.A: Non-determinism describes variability in content or reasoning, not format. The failure here is not that Claude occasionally produces a wrong answer after retries—it is that the prompt never constrained the output structure in the first place, making inconsistent formatting the expected outcome, not an anomaly.
distractor_explanations.C: The parser is not the failure point; it is correctly enforcing the contract the pipeline was designed around. Relaxing the parser to accept free-form prose shifts the problem downstream and does not fix the root cause, which is an underspecified prompt.
distractor_explanations.D: Few-shot examples are one way to guide output format, but explicit format instructions in the system or user prompt are sufficient and often more reliable. The claim that few-shot examples are always required overstates the constraint and misidentifies the actual fix.

correct_explanation: Dynamically injecting expertise level as an explicit constraint gives Claude the specific signal it needs to calibrate vocabulary, depth, and assumed knowledge on every request. This approach scales across the full user spectrum without template proliferation, and explicit constraints outperform implicit reliance on Claude's defaults when audience variation is wide and high-stakes.

distractor_explanations.A: Separate templates solve the calibration problem but fail on scalability — a binary novice/expert split doesn't accommodate the full spectrum of users, and maintaining multiple templates multiplies update overhead whenever the feature changes.
distractor_explanations.C: Instructing Claude to 'explain clearly' without audience context leaves calibration entirely to default behavior, which has no signal to distinguish a confused novice from a condescending response to an engineer — the exact failure mode the team is already experiencing.
distractor_explanations.D: Appending a glossary shifts the cognitive burden onto the user rather than resolving the calibration mismatch in the prompt itself; the core explanation remains mis-targeted for both audiences, and novices are still exposed to confusing language before they reach the glossary.

correct_explanation: Explicit audience constraints act as an early-binding signal that sets the register before Claude interprets the content of the request. Without it, Claude defaults to a middle-ground tone calibrated to an imagined average user, which systematically undershoots for experts and overshoots for novices. The variable injection eliminates that ambiguity by making audience a first-class input rather than an inference.

distractor_explanations.A: This frames the decision as a cost-accuracy tradeoff, but token count is not the reason to keep the placeholder—the failure mode is register mismatch, not accuracy loss. Framing it as a cost argument misidentifies the architectural problem the placeholder solves.
distractor_explanations.C: Claude's inference from question phrasing is unreliable when the phrasing is neutral or when the same question could come from users at different expertise levels. Treating the placeholder as documentation-only ignores that it is a functional calibration signal, as your own A/B test demonstrated.
distractor_explanations.D: No Claude content policy requires prompts to declare an intended audience for explanatory content. This fabricates a compliance rationale that does not exist.

correct_explanation: Positive instructions define the intended output space but don't reliably suppress behaviors Claude has strong generalized tendencies toward—like inferring intent from context. Speculative language is a specific failure mode that requires an explicit negative constraint to block, because the model can satisfy 'summarize only what is stated' while still inserting interpretive framing it judges as helpful. Pairing positive and negative instructions closes this gap by targeting the failure mode directly.

distractor_explanations.A: Increasing specificity in the positive instruction alone is a near-miss: it narrows the target behavior but still doesn't explicitly prohibit the failure mode. Claude can interpret 'verbatim facts and agreed terms' as compatible with brief interpretive glosses, leaving the speculation problem intact.
distractor_explanations.C: Formatting emphasis and instruction placement don't address the underlying cause of the failure. Claude isn't deprioritizing the instruction because it's buried—it's producing speculative language because no constraint prohibits it.
distractor_explanations.D: Replacing positive instructions entirely with prohibitions removes the affirmative behavioral target, leaving Claude without clear guidance on what a correct summary looks like. Effective prompts use both: positive instructions to define the goal, negative constraints to block specific failure modes.

correct_explanation: Positive instructions define the target behavior but leave the solution space open—Claude's generalized capabilities (like inferring intent) can still activate on edge cases because nothing explicitly blocks them. The negative constraint closes that gap by directly prohibiting the specific failure mode the rubric detected. This is why v2 passed: it didn't just reinforce what to do, it suppressed a competing behavior.

distractor_explanations.A: This overgeneralizes from one lab result to a universal rule. The lab demonstrates that negative constraints fixed a specific failure mode in this domain—it does not establish that positive-only prompts always fail across all domains.
distractor_explanations.C: The rubric flagged speculation in v1 and passed v2 after the constraint was added—this is consistent behavior, not unreliability. Attributing the change to rubric sensitivity rather than prompt improvement inverts the actual causal relationship.
distractor_explanations.D: The improvement is attributable to the type of instruction added (a targeted constraint), not prompt length. Longer prompts with irrelevant additions would not fix a specific edge-case failure mode.

correct_explanation: The root failure is rule proliferation without a conflict-resolution mechanism—47 flat rules with no priority ordering guarantee future collisions as edge cases multiply. Refactoring into a principled hierarchy gives Claude an explicit reasoning framework to adjudicate novel conflicts without requiring exhaustive enumeration. This treats the architectural problem (missing priority semantics) rather than the symptom (one specific rule pair).

distractor_explanations.A: Adding rule 48 perpetuates the exact failure mode: each new rule is a patch that creates new potential conflicts with the existing 47, compounding the combinatorial explosion rather than resolving it.
distractor_explanations.C: Replacing rules with examples shifts the conflict from explicit rules to implicit, unauditable learned behavior—this makes regulatory compliance harder to demonstrate and conflict detection impossible, not easier.
distractor_explanations.D: Parallel single-rule classifiers produce vote aggregation across contradictory signals with no principled resolution mechanism; the architectural conflict is displaced into the aggregation layer, not eliminated.

correct_explanation: The failure mode is constraint conflict, not missing examples or insufficient exploration — Claude has no principled basis for choosing between two rules that contradict each other on the same input. An explicit priority hierarchy encodes the resolution logic directly in the prompt architecture, so Claude applies a consistent, generalizable decision rule rather than making arbitrary or index-ordered choices. This addresses the root cause: the constraint block lacks a conflict-resolution mechanism.

distractor_explanations.A: Temperature controls sampling randomness, not rule-following logic — raising it makes output less deterministic but does nothing to resolve the structural ambiguity between conflicting constraints. This treats a prompt architecture problem as a decoding parameter problem.
distractor_explanations.C: Flagging conflicts instead of resolving them shifts the failure from silent to visible but doesn't fix it — downstream code still receives unresolved outputs, and score_response() will continue to degrade. This converts a resolution problem into an escalation problem without addressing the architectural gap.
distractor_explanations.D: Adding per-conflict examples encodes resolutions implicitly and requires a new example every time a new conflict surfaces, making the prompt brittle and O(n) in discovered edge cases. It treats the symptom rather than the structural absence of a priority hierarchy, so previously unseen conflicts remain unresolved.
